objective.subject: English
objective.statement: Identify, construct, punctuate and edit relative clauses, choosing an appropriate relative word or implied pronoun and maintaining clear reference.
misconceptions: Chooses who, which, where, when, whose or that by habit without matching the noun and meaning.
Treats every clause beginning with that as a relative clause or mistakes a question word for a relative word.
Places a relative clause too far from its antecedent, creating unclear or humorous reference.
Adds commas automatically around every relative clause or omits punctuation that helps an embedded clause remain readable.
objective.parent_explanation: Can add and edit relative clauses so extra information sits clearly beside the noun it describes.
objective.teacher_evidence: Stores antecedent choice, relative link, clause placement, punctuation and explanation separately so grammatical understanding is not hidden by spelling, handwriting or working-memory demand.

activities.title: I can add a relative clause that clearly and purposefully tells the reader more about a noun.
activities.prompt: Attach useful information to a noun using a relative link that matches the meaning.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Underline the noun being described and draw an arrow from the extra information back to it.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Activate noun reference before naming relative-clause forms.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Ask which person, thing, place or time the extra clause describes.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Noun phrase and clause cards are joined by a selectable antecedent arrow.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Attach useful information to a noun using a relative link that matches the meaning.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Frame relative clauses as purposeful noun expansion and reference.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: The link may show a person, thing, place, time or possession, and sometimes the pronoun can be implied.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Meaning-labelled links connect noun cards to complete clause cards.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The observatory, which stands above Orbit City, tracks distant storms.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Model an embedded relative clause, clear antecedent and paired punctuation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Which points back to the observatory. The clause is inserted after that noun, and the main sentence remains complete around it.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The main clause forms a complete track while the relative clause docks beside observatory.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Combine two related sentences, place the clause beside its noun, choose the link and reread for meaning and punctuation.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Coordinate sentence combining, antecedent proximity, relative-word choice and fluency.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Keep the intended noun close, remove repeated words and check that the main clause still works.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A COMBINE-PLACE-LINK-REREAD editor preserves both source meanings.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: In 'I know that the door is locked', does that point back to a noun? Compare it with 'the door that is locked'.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Distinguish relative that from other grammatical uses by testing noun reference.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: A relative word introduces a clause that describes a noun. Not every that does this job.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Only the relative example produces a valid arrow to door.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach your companion why you chose the relative link, clause position and punctuation.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Gather metalinguistic evidence and reader-effect reasoning.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Name the antecedent, explain the meaning link and show that the sentence remains clear.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A reference arrow, meaning icon and sentence read-through remain visible during explanation.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A clause that gives more information about a noun or noun phrase.
vocabulary.audio_script: A relative clause points back to a noun and adds information about it.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A clause card connected by an arrow to its noun phrase.

vocabulary.child_explanation: The noun or noun phrase that a relative word points back to.
vocabulary.audio_script: The antecedent is the person, place, time, thing or possession being described.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A labelled noun target receiving a reference arrow.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A word such as who, which, whose or that that links a relative clause to a noun.
vocabulary.audio_script: Choose the relative word that matches the meaning you need.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Meaning gates labelled person, thing, place, time and possession.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A relative pronoun that is understood even though it is not written.
vocabulary.audio_script: Sometimes the linking word can be left out because the relationship stays clear.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A faded that card between a noun and clause.

manipulative.purpose: Make the relationship between antecedents, relative links and clause information explicit.
manipulative.interaction_notes: Learners select a noun phrase, connect a clause, label the relationship as person, thing, place, time or possession and test whether a relative pronoun may be implied.
manipulative.accessibility_notes: Every visual arrow has a text-row equivalent; keyboard controls cycle antecedents and links; relationships are written as words rather than colour codes; read-aloud pauses at clause boundaries; and one link can be shown at a time.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use a calm Orbit City writing desk with static clause cards, one reference arrow at a time and no decorative text movement.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Replace card docking and arrow drawing with numbered noun-clause pairs, persistent brackets and before/after sentence panels.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Read source sentences separately, pause around the relative clause and replay the combined sentence neutrally without exaggerating the answer.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use clause chunking, adjustable spacing, antecedent highlighting with text labels, vocabulary glosses and a plain linear editor.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Use a fixed FIND NOUN-CHOOSE LINK-PLACE CLAUSE-REREAD sequence and reveal one decision at a time.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Remove timers, preserve source sentences, allow oral rehearsal, undo and structured-choice explanations before free writing.
adaptive_support.confidence_support: Credit clear simple sentences, treat clause removal as a valid revision and preserve every correct part during targeted repair.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach person, thing, place, time and possession meanings with paired examples; compare relative patterns with learners' home languages where helpful without assuming direct equivalence.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Identify the antecedent and choose a relative link that accurately expresses person, thing, place, time or possession.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: noun phrase + complete relative clause gap + meaning-labelled link choices + antecedent arrow + oral/typed reason
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use inclusive names and roles, avoid simplistic who-for-people rules where whose or that is also possible, and accept contextually valid alternatives.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Combine two sentences by placing a relative clause next to the intended noun and removing unnecessary repetition.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: two source sentences + repeated noun + end/embedded placement choices + relative link + meaning preservation check
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Allow more than one fluent combination when reference and meaning remain clear; do not reward syntactic complexity by itself.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Distinguish relative that from other uses and recognise where a relative pronoun can be implied.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: paired sentences with that/zero relative/complement use + antecedent test + remove-or-retain trial + explanation
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Teacher review must check grammatical acceptability and avoid teaching omission where the relative pronoun is the clause subject.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Use punctuation to keep embedded additional information readable while attending to meaning and sentence structure.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: main clause + embedded relative clause + punctuation placements + main-clause removal test + read-aloud comparison
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Avoid presenting a universal comma rule for all relative clauses; examples must make the intended meaning sufficiently clear for Year 5 review.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Revise relative clauses for precision, fluency and useful detail rather than adding complexity mechanically.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: narrative or explanation sentence + redundant/vague/overloaded clause + retain/remove/replace/reposition options + reader-effect reason
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Preserve author voice, permit concise main-clause alternatives and ensure style judgements have a clear communicative purpose.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Chooses who, which, where, when, whose or that by habit without matching the noun and meaning.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner selects a grammatically or semantically mismatched link and cannot name the relationship it expresses.
repair_sequence: Label the intended relationship as person, thing, place, time or possession.
Test the relative link in the complete sentence and accept alternatives only if meaning remains accurate.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner selects and explains a suitable relative link in a new context.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Treats every clause beginning with that as a relative clause or mistakes a question word for a relative word.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner labels any occurrence of that, who, where or when as relative without finding an antecedent.
repair_sequence: Search for the noun being described.
Draw a reference arrow from the clause link.
Compare a matched non-relative example and state the different job.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner distinguishes relative and non-relative uses in a fresh sentence pair.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Places a relative clause too far from its antecedent, creating unclear or humorous reference.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The clause can sensibly point to the wrong nearby noun or changes the intended source-sentence meaning.
repair_sequence: Move the clause directly beside it.
Reread and confirm that only one sensible reference arrow remains.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner combines a new sentence pair with clear antecedent proximity.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Adds commas automatically around every relative clause or omits punctuation that helps an embedded clause remain readable.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: The learner applies a memorised comma pattern without checking clause position, intended meaning or main-clause readability.
repair_sequence: Read the sentence with and without the bracketed information.
Choose punctuation that matches the intended embedded information and verify the main clause.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: The learner punctuates a new embedded example and explains the sentence structure rather than citing a universal rule.

feedback.companion_prompt: Teach your companion why you chose the relative link, clause position and punctuation.
feedback.parent_summary: Can add and edit relative clauses so extra information sits clearly beside the noun it describes.
feedback.teacher_evidence: Stores antecedent choice, relative link, clause placement, punctuation and explanation separately so grammatical understanding is not hidden by spelling, handwriting or working-memory demand.
next_actions: Route link errors to person-thing-place-time-possession meaning gates.
Route every-that labelling to antecedent-arrow comparison.
Apply secure relative-clause choices selectively in the learner's own narrative and non-fiction.
Schedule mixed identification, combining and editing retrieval at 1, 3, 7, 14 and 30 days.

body.prompt: Choose the clearest relative word: 'The engineer ___ repaired the rover explained the fault.'

hints: Which word can point back to the engineer as the person doing the repair?
questions.explanation: 'Who repaired the rover' is a relative clause describing the engineer.

body.prompt: Combine the sentences so the relative clause clearly describes the drone. 'The drone entered the tunnel. The drone carried the sensor.'
choices: The drone, which carried the sensor, entered the tunnel.
The drone entered the tunnel, which carried the sensor.
Which carried the sensor the drone entered the tunnel.
The drone entered which the tunnel carried the sensor.

expected_answer.value: The drone, which carried the sensor, entered the tunnel.

hints: Place the extra information next to the drone.
questions.explanation: Placing 'which carried the sensor' directly after 'the drone' makes the intended antecedent clear.

body.prompt: Which sentence uses 'that' to begin a relative clause describing a noun?
choices: The message that arrived at noon contained the code.
I think that the message arrived at noon.

expected_answer.value: The message that arrived at noon contained the code.

hints: Find the sentence where the clause after 'that' describes a noun.
Can you draw an arrow from 'that' back to 'the message'?
questions.explanation: In the first sentence, 'that arrived at noon' describes 'the message', so it is a relative clause.

body.prompt: Punctuate the embedded relative clause: 'The observatory which stands above Orbit City tracks distant storms.'
choices: The observatory, which stands above Orbit City, tracks distant storms.
The observatory which, stands above Orbit City tracks distant storms.
The observatory, which stands above Orbit City tracks, distant storms.
The observatory which stands, above Orbit City, tracks distant storms.

expected_answer.value: The observatory, which stands above Orbit City, tracks distant storms.

hints: The extra clause is inserted after 'the observatory'.
Use a matching pair of commas around the inserted information.
questions.explanation: The paired commas mark the embedded additional information, while 'The observatory tracks distant storms' remains the main clause.

reward_payload.explanation: Can add and edit relative clauses so extra information sits clearly beside the noun it describes.
reward_payload.companion_prompt: Teach your companion why you chose the relative link, clause position and punctuation.
